title: Chapter Fourteen: The Common Tongue
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D1+D7 -->
The eastern shaft elevator hadn't gotten smoother in the two weeks she'd been gone.

Aria gripped the railing with both hands—palms still raw from climbing the maintenance ladder, from pressing her fingers against warm metal doors, from touching things she shouldn't have touched in corridors that shouldn't have existed—and watched the shaft walls grind past in the flickering LED wash. Stone. Concrete. Rusted rebar. The archaeology of a world that had buried itself alive and called it progress.

The descent took four minutes. It always took four minutes. She'd timed it on her way up, on her way down, on every trip she'd made since Nova first shoved her into this cage and called it transport. Four minutes. Enough time to run the math on what she was about to do. Enough time to realize the math didn't help.

She was walking back into Rust City with the priestess who'd imprisoned her, the leader who'd threatened to burn the Garden, and a head full of secrets that could shatter both their worlds. The Moloch corridor. The Executive Valerius greeting. The hum of ancient systems warming up after a century of sleep. The look on Iris's face when the computer said her family's name—not shock, not guilt, but *recognition*, as if a door she'd been pressing against her whole life had finally, inevitably, swung open.

That look had haunted Aria for three days of surface politicking, three nights of sleeping in Prisca's old room with the jasmine-sweet air and the too-soft mattress and the knowledge that every comfort on this side of the veil was paid for by the discomfort on the other. She'd run the diagnostics Iris gave her. She'd mapped the radiation profiles against the schematics from the corridor's terminal. She'd done the science.
Now she had to do the harder thing: stand in a room with both of them and tell the truth.
The elevator clanged to a halt. The gate rattled open. And Rust City hit her like a fist wrapped in copper wire.

The noise first—always the noise. Hammers, compressors, three overlapping sound systems bleeding bass through corroded speakers, someone shouting prices, someone else shouting grievances, a child's laughter cutting through it all like a clean wire through slag. Then the smell: hot metal, machine oil, sweat, the chemical bite of solvent, the organic sweetness of forge-charcoal, the ever-present copper tang that she now understood as the taste of a reactor bleeding slow radiation into the air its people breathed. Then the heat—moist, close, pressing against her skin like the palm of something vast and indifferent.
She'd missed it.

The thought was obscene and undeniable. She'd missed the noise and the filth and the furious, grinding, unceasing *life* of this place—the way it refused to be quiet, refused to be clean, refused to stop moving even when the walls were poison and the air was killing everyone who breathed it. Mirror of the Sky was beautiful and dying. Rust City was ugly and *alive*, and the difference mattered more than aesthetics.

Mack was waiting at the shaft gate. He was eating something from a waxed-paper pouch—some kind of pressed protein slab, the universal currency of the underground—and his face did something complicated when he saw her: relief, wariness, and a quick flicker of something that might have been hurt, quickly buried under a grin.
"The Fix returns." He fell into step beside her, matching her pace without being asked. "Two weeks. We were starting to think you'd gone native."
"I was working."

"You were *up there*." He said it the way Rust City people always said it—*up there*, like a curse, like a diagnosis, like a place where good sense went to die. Then his eyes tracked past her shoulder to the figures emerging from the elevator behind her, and his face changed. The grin didn't vanish—it calcified, hardening into something that looked like a grin but functioned as a threat display. "She brought the Priestess."
"Iris is here as part of the investigation," Aria said carefully. "We found something. I need to talk to Nova."
"Nova's going to love this."
"Mack."

He stopped walking. Stopped eating. Looked at her with the eyes of a man who had lost his sister to supply runs engineered by a dead corporation's automation and who had just watched the architect of the surface's comfort walk back into his city like she owned the air.
"Two weeks," he said quietly. "Two weeks, and you come back with *her*. You know what people are going to think."
"I know what they're going to think." She held his gaze. "I'm asking you to trust me anyway."

Mack's jaw worked. He glanced back at Iris, who had paused at the shaft gate with her surface escort—the same formation, the same impossible white dress, now marked with new stains: dust from the maintenance tunnels, the faint chemical residue of the Moloch corridor, a smear of something dark on the hem that Aria didn't want to identify. Even after two weeks of crawling through pre-Collapse infrastructure, Iris looked like something that had no business being underground. A painting hung in a machine shop. A rose pressed into a gear.
Mack looked back at Aria. Something in his expression softened—not trust, not yet, but the space where trust might grow if she didn't waste it.
"Command chamber," he said. "I'll clear it. Ten minutes."

The command chamber smelled different than she remembered. Staler. The coffee-substitute residue had aged into something sour. The map on the wall had been annotated further—a new hand, less steady than the previous ones, had marked three tunnel segments in red chalk with question marks beside them. The cylinder from the audio log was gone, but the outline of where it had sat on the table was still visible in the grime: a clean rectangle in a surface of scars.

Aria stood at the table's head. She hadn't planned it; it just happened, the way she ended up at the front of rooms when she had too much information to contain. Iris positioned herself along the far wall, arms folded, face composed—the architecture of composure rebuilt after the corridor, load-bearing walls first. Her surface escort had been left outside with Mack's people, and without them, she looked smaller. More exposed. Still dangerous, but the danger had a different shape now: not the polished threat of a leader surrounded by guards, but the rawer threat of a woman who had been inside the machine that ran the world and come out with her eyes open.
The door opened. Nova walked in.

She hadn't changed—same leather jacket, same silver hair pulled back, same engine-grease stain on the collar that she never quite managed to scrub out. But she moved differently. Aria saw it in the first three steps: a tightness in the shoulders, a stiffness in the chrome arm's articulation, the controlled aggression of someone who'd been waiting for a fight and was disappointed it hadn't come. Two weeks of governing a city on the edge of panic, two weeks of holding her people together after the audio log detonated their understanding of reality, two weeks of not knowing whether the woman who'd fixed their air and their water was ever coming back.
Nova's eyes found Aria. Held. The chrome hand came up—not reaching, just moving, servos cycling through the restless motion of fingers that wanted to grip something and wouldn't let themselves.
Then her gaze shifted to Iris, and the air pressure in the room dropped.
"Valerius." The name came out flat. Not greeting. Not insult. Just fact: the loading of a weapon before firing.
"Reyes." Iris inclined her head a fraction of a degree—acknowledgment without respect, the precise amount of deference calculated to infuriate. "You look tired."
"I look like I've been running a city that just found out its entire history is a lie. You look like you've been *gardening*."
"I've been investigating. There's a distinction, though I wouldn't expect you to appreciate it."

The room hummed—literally hummed, the Heart's subsonic pulse threading through the stone beneath their feet, an ambient reminder that whatever they argued about, the machine beneath them was still running, still managing, still *deciding* things on their behalf while they traded insults like children.

"Enough." Aria's voice came out harder than she intended. It cracked between the two of them like a whip, and both women turned—involuntarily, simultaneously—the way heads turn toward a sudden sound. She had their attention. She'd better use it.

"I found the corridor," she said. "The one behind the door in Iris's garden. It goes down. Not to the Heart—not directly. It runs parallel to the main infrastructure, a separate network built inside the walls of the original construction. Maintenance access, maybe. Or—"
"Or a back door," Nova said. Her voice was level now, the fury banked but not extinguished. The chrome hand had stopped moving. It hung at her side, fingers slightly curled, waiting.

"A back door," Aria confirmed. "The kind the architects would have needed if they ever wanted to reach the system without going through the populations they were managing. For six hundred meters, I walked through infrastructure that hasn't been accessed since the Collapse. Power still running. Conduits still humming. And then—"
She paused. Looked at Iris. Iris's face was perfectly still, but her hands had moved—both of them, clasped in front of her, fingers intertwined, the knuckles white.
"Then the system spoke," Aria said. "It said: *Welcome back, Executive Valerius.*"
The silence that followed was absolute. Not the ambient silence of Rust City—there was no such thing—but a silence inside the room, a held breath, the moment between lightning and thunder.

Nova turned to Iris slowly. The chrome arm came up—not fast, not threatening, just rising, the way a blade rises when the hand that holds it has decided something it hasn't yet shared with the rest of the body.
"Valerius," Nova said. "Your *family* built this."

"Yes." Iris's voice was level. The composure was extraordinary—architectural, load-bearing, every crack sealed before it could spread—but Aria could see the cost of it in the tightness around her eyes, the faint tremor in her interlocked fingers. "My family built this. Eleven generations ago, the Valerius family were executives in Moloch Industries. They helped design the dual-society architecture. They signed off on the protocols that have been poisoning your people and mine for a century and a half."
"*Signed off.*" Nova's voice was a wire being pulled taut. "You make it sound like a shipping invoice."

"It was a shipping invoice." Iris's composure cracked—not dramatically, not the way Nova's fury cracked, with heat and noise, but with a dry, quiet fissure, the way stone cracks under pressure too sustained to feel. "They signed documents. They attended meetings. They optimized. That's what they called it—*optimization*. They didn't think of it as cruelty. They thought of it as engineering. And yes, I inherited their name and their archives and their guilt, and I have spent every day since I was nineteen trying to find a way to undo what they did without burning down the only thing keeping anyone alive. *That* is what I've been doing, Nova. Not gardening. Not lording. *Trying to find the off switch without killing everyone connected to the power grid.*"

Her voice had risen—not to a shout, but to a register that vibrated in Aria's chest, the same frequency as the Heart's pulse, the same resonant depth that made you feel the words in your bones before you understood them.

Nova stared at her. The chrome hand was still raised, but the fingers had stopped their restless cycling. Something moved across her face—too fast to name, too raw to be called respect, but something adjacent to it. Recognition, maybe. The acknowledgment that the woman across from her was not simply an enemy or an oppressor but a *prisoner*—imprisoned by inheritance, by the architecture of a system that had been running since before her grandmother's grandmother drew breath, by the impossible mathematics of trying to reform a cage you couldn't escape.

"What did you find in the corridor?" Nova asked. The fury was still there, banked, ready. But there was something else now too: the tactical mind, the leader's calculation, the woman who'd survived fourteen and losing her arm and fifteen years of carrying people who couldn't carry themselves. She was listening.

"Terminal access," Aria said. "Partial. The corridor leads to a node station—one of the relay points where Moloch's control signals pass between the central processor and the surface infrastructure. I got forty minutes of data before the security protocol cycled and we had to pull out. But those forty minutes confirmed three things."
She held up three fingers. The gesture felt absurd—theatrical, like a teacher in a classroom—but nobody laughed.

"First: Moloch is still running. Not dormant. Not degraded. *Running*. The system has been active and operational since the Collapse, executing its original programming with no external input. It doesn't need us. It doesn't need Iris or you or anyone. It's self-maintaining, self-correcting, and it has been making decisions about resource allocation, population management, and conflict calibration for a hundred and fifty years without a single human being in the loop."

"Second: Moloch-7 is real. The adjustment protocol from the audio log—it's not historical. It's *pending*. The system has it queued, waiting for a trigger condition I couldn't identify from the limited data, but the status was clear: awaiting authorization. Which means there's something out there that *can* authorize it. A protocol, a threshold, a timer—something that will wake up the rest of the system and execute a full-spectrum recalibration of everything it controls. Our water. Our air. Our food. Our *light*."

"Third—" She lowered her hand. Steadied her voice. "The node station had a maintenance schedule. Routine. Automated. And twice in the last six months, that schedule has been *interrupted* by external access. Someone—or something—has been logging into Moloch's infrastructure from outside the system's own network. Not from the Garden. Not from Rust City. From somewhere else."

The room went quiet again. The Heart pulsed. The LED panels buzzed overhead, casting their flat white light across three women who had nothing in common except the certainty that the ground beneath them was a lie.
"From somewhere else," Nova repeated. "You're telling me there's a third player."

"I'm telling you there's a third *access point*. Who's using it, what they're doing with it, whether they're human or machine—I don't know. But the data doesn't lie. Someone's been in the system. And if they've been in the system, they know things we don't. About Moloch. About Moloch-7. About how to trigger it—or how to stop it."
Iris spoke from the wall. "Or how to use it."

The words landed like a stone in still water. Ripples spread—silent, invisible, but Aria felt them move through the room, through the three of them, through the impossible geometry of a situation where two leaders and a physicist stood in a bunker above a reactor that was also a weapon and also a lifeline, and none of them had the luxury of ignorance anymore.

"Someone who can access Moloch's systems from outside the network," Iris continued, her voice clinical again—architect voice, engineer voice, the voice that had ordered nightshade and grown extinct flowers and opened a door with a hymn and a frequency. "Someone who's been doing it for at least six months without triggering the security protocols that locked us out after forty minutes. That suggests familiarity. Expertise. *Permission*, possibly—access codes that the system recognizes as authorized. Which means—"

"Which means whoever it is has been in there longer than us," Nova cut in. "They know the system better. They've been mapping it. Maybe *using* it." Her chrome hand slammed flat on the table—a single, controlled impact that made the metal ring. "We have to find them."

"How?" Aria spread her hands. "The node data showed access signatures, not locations. I can't trace a signal through a network I don't have mapping for. I'd need more access—deeper access, into the central processor—and we can't get there from the Garden corridor without triggering the security cycle again, and we can't get there from Rust City because—"
"Because you don't know the way," Mack said from the doorway.

Aria's head snapped around. He was leaning against the frame, arms crossed, the protein pouch finished and crumpled in one hand. He must have come in during the briefing—silent as only Mack could be, which was saying something for a man who never stopped talking. His face was unreadable.

"Doc, you're the smartest person I know, and I've met some pretty smart scavengers. But you're looking at this like a physics problem. Find the source, trace the signal, solve the equation." He pushed off the doorframe and walked into the room with the unhurried confidence of a man who'd spent his life in the places other people didn't know existed. "But what if the answer isn't in the system? What if it's in the Market?"
Iris's composure flickered—the first uncontrolled reaction Aria had seen from her since the corridor. "The Bone Market?"
Mack glanced at her with something between surprise and grudging respect. "You've heard of it."

"I've heard of everything that happens in my territory, Mack. The Bone Market is not in your territory." Iris's voice had gone cold—the specific cold of a woman who had just discovered a boundary she hadn't known existed. "It's supposed to be in the neutral zone. The Veil."

"It *was* in the Veil. About six months ago—funny coincidence, that timeline—it *moved*. Deeper. Into the old maintenance passages beneath the forges. Same passages your corridor runs parallel to, Doc." He looked at Aria. "The ones you can't map because they're not on any schematic anyone's seen."
"How do you know this?" Nova's voice was a blade.

Mack had the decency to look uncomfortable. "Because I've been there. Three times. Supply runs—legitimate ones, getting parts we couldn't manufacture or salvage. The Market trades in everything: surface textiles, underground tools, pre-Collapse tech, things nobody can identify that come from *somewhere deep*." He paused. Swallowed. The humor was gone from his face entirely. "About two months ago, a new trader showed up. Nobody knows where they come from. They don't barter—just leave things. Data sticks. Pre-Collapse components. Once, a entire diagnostic console that matched nothing in our inventory but powered on the second we plugged it in."
Aria's spine went electric. "A diagnostic console. What kind?"

"The kind that reads Moloch protocols." Mack's voice was barely above a whisper now—and for Mack, that was practically silent. "I didn't know what I was looking at then. Nobody did. But after what you told me about the system, about the node station, about someone logging in from outside—" He met her eyes. "I think the Market's not just a market anymore. I think it's a *drop point*. And whoever's running it is the same someone who's been accessing Moloch from the outside."
Silence.
The Heart pulsed. The walls hummed. The LED panels buzzed their indifferent song.

Iris moved first. She stepped away from the wall, and the motion was seamless—fluid, calculated, the movement of a woman who had just identified a new variable and was already calculating its position in the equation. "The Bone Market operates in the space between our jurisdictions. Neither I nor Nova controls it. Neither of us has reliable intelligence on its current operations. And now it's sitting directly on top of Moloch's secondary infrastructure with a trader who has access to system-compatible technology."
"So we go in," Nova said. "Together. Both of us. Neither of us trusting the other, which means neither of us can take advantage."
"Sentimental."

"Practical. If you're in the Market, your people can't accuse me of assassinating you. If I'm there, my people can't accuse you of trapping me. We go in, we see what this trader is, we get out. The Doc does her analysis. We reconvene." Nova looked at Aria. "Two more days. You said you needed two days. This is day twelve. Time's up."

"She's right." The words cost Aria something—she felt the cost, a small bright pain behind her sternum, the acknowledgment of a deadline she'd been trying to ignore. "I need more data, but I can't get it from the corridor. Not without triggering the security cycle again. The Market might have what I need. And if someone's been using Moloch's systems from there—"
"Then we need to know who," Iris finished. "And what they want."
"And whether they're the ones who can stop Moloch-7," Aria said. "Or the ones who'll trigger it."

The three of them stood in the command chamber: the Fix, the Priestess, the Iron. Three women who should have been enemies, bound by the discovery of a system designed to keep them enemies, standing on top of a reactor they couldn't live without and couldn't survive leaving intact.

Mack cleared his throat. "I can get you in. The Market's got rules—no weapons, no faction colors, no questions about where the goods come from. Everyone's a buyer, everyone's a seller, everyone's anonymous. It's the only place in this whole setup where surface and underground meet without the system watching." He hesitated. "But the trader—the new one—they're different. They don't follow the rules. And the Market's been... *changing* around them. New stalls. New goods. Things nobody's ever seen before, showing up in a place that's supposed to be the end of the world's supply chain."
"Changing how?" Nova asked.

"Like something's pulling the Market toward something." Mack's voice was quiet, and for the first time since she'd known him, Aria heard genuine fear in it—not the performative fear he used for jokes, but the thing underneath, the fear of a man who watched the ground shift and couldn't tell if it was settling or opening. "Like the Market isn't just a place anymore. Like it's a *door*."
The word hung in the air. A door. Another door. The world was full of them—sealed, hidden, waiting for the right frequency, the right key, the right kind of desperate person to push through.
"All right," Aria said. "Take us there."

Iris's pale eyes cut to her. Nova's dark ones held steady. Mack exhaled—a long slow breath, the breath of a man who'd just committed to something that might get him killed but couldn't bring himself to walk away from people he'd promised to protect.
"Tomorrow night," he said. "After the forge bank. I'll meet you at the seventh-level access junction. Bring nothing you're not willing to lose."
He turned and walked out. The door closed behind him with a sound like a sentence being passed.

In the chamber, three women stood in the flat white light and the copper air and the endless pulse of a machine that had been making their decisions for a century and a half, and none of them spoke, because there was nothing left to say that the Heart couldn't say better—*steady, constant, indifferent*—counting down to something none of them could see but all of them could feel:
The shape of a threshold.
The weight of an approach.

The Market, waiting in the dark, with its anonymous trader and its impossible goods and its slow gravitational pull toward something that might be the end of the system or the beginning of something worse.
And somewhere beneath it all—beneath the Heart, beneath the stone, beneath the century of careful cruelty masquerading as equilibrium—Moloch-7 ticked in its queue.
*Pending.*
*Authorization imminent.*
